title: The pharmacist who counted birds
summary: A retired pharmacist in north Wales spent fourteen years indexing the dawn calls heard from his garden — and what he discovered surprised the people who eventually noticed.
body:
Owain Williams retired from a small pharmacy in Caernarfon in 2009, at sixty-six. He did not intend to start an unpaid second career. He intended to read, to walk, and to fix the back garden. What changed his plans was the dawn chorus.

Owain's garden backed onto a hedge with an oak, two ash, and a tangle of bramble. In his first retired spring, he noticed that he could hear, from the kitchen, at least eight different bird songs before seven in the morning. He began to write them down on the back of a calendar. By summer, the calendar had ninety-six entries. By winter, he had bought a small notebook. By the following spring, he had bought a microphone.

For fourteen years, Owain has recorded the dawn chorus from the same kitchen window. He has logged date, time, weather, and the order in which species call. He uses a system he invented — three letters for each species, a single digit for confidence, a small mark for songs he could not place. The notebooks now fill a wooden shelf he built for them. There are sixteen of them.

He did not show the notebooks to anyone for ten years. Then, in 2019, a researcher from Bangor University asked at the local library whether anyone in the area kept long-term bird notes. The librarian put her in touch with Owain. The researcher visited twice, then four times, then asked permission to digitise the notebooks for a project on chorus shifts caused by warmer springs.

The results were modest but useful. Owain's records showed that, on his street, the song thrush had been starting its dawn song an average of eleven minutes earlier in 2022 than in 2009. The blackbird had shifted by six minutes. The robin had not shifted at all. Three species he had logged in his first four years — including a rare warbler — had not been heard in his garden since 2017. None of these findings would have been provable without the notebooks. The researcher named Owain as second author on a small academic paper.

Owain has been asked, since the paper appeared, what made him keep counting. He has said he liked the order. He liked knowing, at quarter to five on a damp April morning, which bird he was likely to hear first. He has said he did not think of himself as doing science. He thought of himself as keeping a tidy house. The science, he has said, was somebody else's word for what he was already doing.

annotations.meaningVi: hợp xướng rạng đông (chim hót đồng loạt lúc bình minh)
bụi cây mâm xôi rậm rạp
đã ghi lại / ghi nhật ký
không xác định được (là loài gì)
đã dịch chuyển / đã thay đổi
căn nhà gọn gàng (ở đây: thói quen sống ngăn nắp)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for ongoing actions: 'has recorded… for fourteen years'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Present perfect ('has/have + V3') diễn tả một hành động bắt đầu trong quá khứ và tiếp tục đến hiện tại, đặc biệt với 'for + khoảng thời gian' hoặc 'since + mốc'. Trong bài: 'For fourteen years, Owain has recorded the dawn chorus from the same kitchen window.' — bắt đầu 14 năm trước và vẫn đang làm. 'He has said he liked the order.' — cũng present perfect, vì ông đã nói câu này nhiều lần và còn đứng vững. So sánh: 'He recorded for fourteen years' (past simple) sẽ ngầm rằng ông đã ngừng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Present perfect ('has/have + V3') marks an action that began in the past and continues to now, especially with 'for + duration' or 'since + start point'. In the passage: 'For fourteen years, Owain has recorded the dawn chorus from the same kitchen window.' — began 14 years ago and continues. 'He has said he liked the order.' — also present perfect, because he has said it more than once and it still stands. Compare 'He recorded for fourteen years' (past simple), which would imply he has stopped.

translationVi:
Owain Williams nghỉ hưu khỏi một tiệm thuốc nhỏ ở Caernarfon năm 2009, sáu mươi sáu tuổi. Ông không định bắt đầu sự nghiệp không lương thứ hai. Ông định đọc, đi bộ, sửa vườn sau. Cái thay đổi kế hoạch là hợp xướng rạng đông.

Vườn Owain giáp lưng vào hàng rào có một cây sồi, hai cây tần bì, và một bụi mâm xôi rậm rạp. Mùa xuân nghỉ hưu đầu tiên, ông nhận thấy có thể nghe, từ bếp, ít nhất tám tiếng chim hót khác nhau trước bảy giờ sáng. Ông bắt đầu ghi vào mặt sau một tờ lịch. Đến hè, tờ lịch có chín mươi sáu mục. Đến đông, ông mua một cuốn sổ nhỏ. Đến xuân sau, ông mua một micrô.

Mười bốn năm qua, Owain ghi âm hợp xướng rạng đông từ cùng một cửa sổ bếp. Ông ghi ngày, giờ, thời tiết, và thứ tự các loài hót. Ông dùng hệ thống tự đặt — ba chữ cái cho mỗi loài, một chữ số cho độ tin cậy, một dấu nhỏ cho tiếng không xác định được. Các sổ giờ đầy một kệ gỗ ông tự đóng cho chúng. Mười sáu cuốn.

Ông không cho ai xem trong mười năm. Rồi, 2019, một nhà nghiên cứu từ Đại học Bangor hỏi tại thư viện địa phương xem có ai trong vùng giữ ghi chép chim dài hạn không. Thủ thư kết nối cô với Owain. Nhà nghiên cứu thăm hai lần, rồi bốn lần, rồi xin phép số hoá các sổ cho một dự án về dịch chuyển hợp xướng do mùa xuân ấm hơn.

Kết quả khiêm tốn nhưng hữu ích. Ghi chép của Owain cho thấy, trên phố ông, chim hoét hót đã bắt đầu hót sớm hơn trung bình mười một phút năm 2022 so với 2009. Chim sáo đen dịch sáu phút. Chim cổ đỏ không dịch chút nào. Ba loài ông ghi trong bốn năm đầu — gồm một loài chích hiếm — không nghe thấy trong vườn ông từ 2017. Không có sổ thì không chứng minh được. Nhà nghiên cứu để Owain là tác giả thứ hai của một bài báo học thuật nhỏ.

Ông được hỏi, từ khi bài báo xuất hiện, điều gì khiến ông cứ đếm. Ông nói thích sự trật tự. Thích biết, lúc bốn giờ bốn lăm sáng tháng Tư ẩm, loài chim nào ông có khả năng nghe đầu tiên. Ông nói không nghĩ mình đang làm khoa học. Ông nghĩ mình đang giữ nhà gọn gàng. Khoa học, ông nói, là từ người khác đặt cho việc ông đã làm.

questions.prompt: Who is Owain, when did he retire, and from what?
What did he originally plan to do?
options: Read, walk, fix the back garden
An oak, two ash, a tangle of bramble

questions.prompt: What tools did he progress through?
options: Calendar back, then a notebook, then a microphone
Three letters per species, a digit for confidence, a mark for unidentified songs

questions.prompt: How did the researcher find him?
options: She asked at the library; the librarian put her in touch

questions.prompt: By how many minutes had the song thrush shifted earlier?
What had disappeared from his garden by 2017?
options: Three species, including a rare warbler

questions.prompt: How does Owain describe what he was doing?
options: Keeping a tidy house — 'science' was somebody else's word for it

questions.prompt: Which is NOT in the passage?
options: He kept the system to himself for ten years
He is named as second author
questions.explanation: He did not sell them; he allowed digitisation.

options: A retired person's quiet, orderly habit becomes scientifically valuable only when someone else noticed — but the meaning to him was always the order itself

He uses a system he invented — three letters for each species, a single digit for {{b5}}, a small mark for songs he could not place. The notebooks now fill a wooden shelf he built for them. There are sixteen of them.

He did not show the notebooks to anyone for ten years. Then, in 2019, a researcher from Bangor University asked at the local library whether anyone in the area kept long-term bird notes. The librarian put her in touch with Owain. The researcher visited twice, then four times, then asked permission to {{b6}} the notebooks for a project on chorus shifts caused by warmer springs.

The results were modest but useful. Owain's records showed that, on his street, the song thrush had been starting its dawn song an average of eleven minutes earlier in 2022 than in 2009. The blackbird had shifted by {{b7}} minutes. The robin had not shifted at all. Three species he had logged in his first four years — including a rare {{b8}} — had not been heard in his garden since 2017. None of these findings would have been provable without the notebooks. The researcher named Owain as second author on a small academic paper.

Owain has been asked, since the paper appeared, what made him keep counting. He has said he liked the {{b9}}. He liked knowing, at quarter to five on a damp April morning, which bird he was likely to hear first. He has said he did not think of himself as doing science. He thought of himself as keeping a tidy {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Owain calls his work 'keeping a tidy house' rather than science. Is the value of his notebooks in what they later proved, or in the daily habit that produced them? Which would you say is the more honest answer, and why?
